topic: The USA c1920-55: Boom, Bust & Recovery
subject: history

items.q: Did the New Deal end the Great Depression?
items.correct: It eased suffering and reduced unemployment but did not fully cure the economy; recovery came mainly with wartime production after 1941.
items.misconception: The New Deal completely ended the Depression by itself.
items.random_wrong: The New Deal caused the Wall Street Crash.
items.why: Students overstate the New Deal's economic results; it provided relief and reform, but full recovery awaited the wartime boom.

items.q: Why did the 1920s boom collapse in 1929?
items.correct: Overproduction, speculation, unequal wealth and risky credit made the stock market unstable, leading to the Wall Street Crash.
items.misconception: The boom collapsed because of the Second World War.
items.random_wrong: The crash happened because Roosevelt was elected.
items.why: Learners misorder events; the crash preceded the war and Roosevelt's presidency, caused by underlying economic weaknesses.

items.q: How did Hoover respond to the Depression?
items.correct: Hoover favoured limited intervention and self-help, which proved inadequate, contributing to his defeat by Roosevelt in 1932.
items.misconception: Hoover launched the New Deal to fight the Depression.
items.random_wrong: Hoover was a socialist who nationalised industry.
items.why: Students attribute the New Deal to Hoover; it was Roosevelt's programme, and Hoover's reluctance to intervene damaged his reputation.

items.q: What name is given to the US economic boom of the 1920s?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Roaring Twenties. “The New Deal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What event in October 1929 triggered the Great Depression?
items.why: It's The Wall Street Crash. “Pearl Harbor” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who introduced the New Deal to fight the Depression?
items.why: It's Franklin Roosevelt. “Herbert Hoover” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What finally ended the Great Depression in the USA?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Wartime industrial production. “The Wall Street Crash” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Wall Street Crash occur?
items.why: Exact answer: 1929 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
